peek() on 3D datasets ","user":{"login":"mireianievas","id":2607760,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MDc3NjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2607760?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas","html_url":"https://github.com/mireianievas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":3162577242,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMTYyNTc3MjQy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/labels/feature-request","name":"feature-request","color":"d93f0b","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-15T12:17:01Z","updated_at":"2024-11-15T12:17:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As of now, the peek on a 3D dataset (e.g. from Fermi-LAT) gives something like this

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f033481a-f666-46f1-8ee1-b9ed19fa3606)

This is not very nice, and is rather incomplete as it shows very little information about the IRFs (only the exposure). 

For this work https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.20487 I built something a little bit more informative

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a684ed1a-180f-43a7-8d50-c24ce60b87d3)

That shows 1D and 2D projections of basically all the IRFs. The only missing part would be possibly the background, which for Fermi-LAT is None (it is included as part of the modelling) so I skipped it.

Maybe something in this line could be considered to replace what peek() does in this case? when np.isnan is called on strings (modelings.models.core, writing a model to disk)","user":{"login":"mireianievas","id":2607760,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MDc3NjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2607760?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas","html_url":"https://github.com/mireianievas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":39740769,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzOTc0MDc2OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"fc2929","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-17T09:29:03Z","updated_at":"2024-07-17T09:29:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version**
1.2 
numpy version 1.26.4

**Bug description**
np.isnan(string) TypeError triggered when running this block in modelings.models.core:

```
                    if par[item] == default or (
                        np.isnan(par[item]) and np.isnan(default)
                    ):
                        del par[item]

```

Error is

```
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

**Expected behavior**
I think a check should be introduced right before to avoid doing that np.isnan in case the par[item] object is a string.

**To Reproduce**
The error popped up while trying to save a Fermi model containing interp = 'lin' strings. 

**Other information**

version**
v1.0

**Bug description**
Not using the very last version of gammapy, but probably nothing changed between 1.0 and 1.1 to fix this (correct me if I am wrong). 

This is actually a double-bug. 

At least with Fermi, if you save the model for one source (from a skymodel with many) into a file, then load it back with Models.from_yaml() and plot it, the result is different than the original one. I am suspecting the covariance matrix (not saved for the one-source-writing-case) has to do something with it, but I am not fully sure. If that is the case, I'm not sure if there is an easy way to warn the user of that missing covariance matrix? maybe while saving the model inserting some kind of flag that this is a component from a complex model ? Otherwise it is quite dangerous to be honest. 

In any case, saving the entire skymodel does not seem to work either, as the to_yaml() makes a call to to_dict() which checks for nan values in the skymodel. For Fermi, there are several spatial parameters (lon_0, lat_0, etc) with nans in the min, max for instance. The error while saving the full model in that case is

```
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

**Expected behavior**
I would expect to get the same plot, and to be able to write the model to disk despite the nan values in some parameters.

**To Reproduce**
```
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

plot_kwargs = {
    "energy_bounds": [1*u.GeV,300*u.GeV], 
    "sed_type": "e2dnde",
    "yunits": u.Unit("erg cm-2 s-1"),
    "xunits": u.GeV,
    "ax": ax,
}

LAT_FullDataset.model['mysource'].spectral_model.plot_error(**plot_kwargs,
                                         label='LAT Full Dataset',
                                         ec='C0',facecolor='white',hatch='+++++++',alpha=0.5,zorder=-10)

myloadedmodel = Models.from_yaml(Models(LAT_FullDataset.model['mysource']).to_yaml())
myloadedmodel['mysource'].spectral_model.plot_error(**plot_kwargs,
                                         label='LAT Full Dataset',
                                         ec='C3',facecolor='white',hatch='\\\\\\\\\\',alpha=0.5,zorder=-10)

```

![image](https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/assets/2607760/41602e2b-5a64-48f0-ad22-3ca0d350d162)

version**
1.01

**Bug description**
When plotting the pointings, plot_spectrum_datasets_off_regions tries to pop 'color' but color might not be in the kwargs dict

**Expected behavior**
an if should exist in place in gammapy/visualization/utils.py

```
 87     for props, dataset in zip(prop_cycle(), datasets):
 88         plot_kwargs = kwargs.copy()
 89         plot_kwargs["facecolor"] = "None"
 90         if 'color' in props:
 91             plot_kwargs.setdefault("edgecolor", props.pop("color"))
```

**To Reproduce**
Run the energy-dependent spectral analysis notebook:

```
plt.figure()
ax = counts.plot(cmap="viridis")
geom.plot_region(ax=ax, kwargs_point={"color": "k", "marker": "*"})
plot_spectrum_datasets_off_regions(ax=ax, datasets=datasets)
```

version**
1.0.1

**Bug description**
LightCurveEstimator fails when I set Reoptimize = True (it works with it as False), saying my model has no free parameters (but amplitude is free).

**Expected behavior**
my model contains amplitude as a free parameter, so this error should not be triggered

**To Reproduce**

```
from gammapy.estimators import LightCurveEstimator
from astropy.time import Time


lc_maker_1d = LightCurveEstimator(
    time_intervals = bins,
    energy_edges=[emin, emax] * u.TeV, source="mysource",reoptimize=True,
)

# this model copy is not necessary, but make explicit we do not freeze the amplitude
model_lc = datasets_lc.models[0].copy()
model_lc.spectral_model.parameters['index'].frozen = True
model_lc.spectral_model.parameters['amplitude'].frozen = False
model_lc._name = 'M87'
datasets_lc.models = [model_lc]

lc_1d = lc_maker_1d.run(datasets_lc)

display(lc_1d.to_table(sed_type="flux", format="lightcurve"))
```

LightCurveEstimator fails when I set Reoptimize = True (it works with it as False), saying my model has no free parameters (but amplitude is free).

**Expected behavior**
my model contains amplitude as a free parameter, so this error should not be triggered

**To Reproduce**

```
from gammapy.estimators import LightCurveEstimator
from astropy.time import Time


lc_maker_1d = LightCurveEstimator(
    time_intervals = bins,
    energy_edges=[emin, emax] * u.TeV, source="mysource",reoptimize=True,
)

# this model copy is not necessary, but make explicit we do not freeze the amplitude
model_lc = datasets_lc.models[0].copy()
model_lc.spectral_model.parameters['index'].frozen = True
model_lc.spectral_model.parameters['amplitude'].frozen = False
model_lc._name = 'mysource'
datasets_lc.models = [model_lc]

lc_1d = lc_maker_1d.run(datasets_lc)

display(lc_1d.to_table(sed_type="flux", format="lightcurve"))
```

version**
v1.0

**Bug description**
Not using the very last version of gammapy, but probably nothing changed between 1.0 and 1.1 to fix this (correct me if I am wrong). 

At least with Fermi, if you save the model for one source (from a skymodel with many) into a file, then load it back with Models.from_yaml() and plot it, the result is different than the original one. I am suspecting the covariance matrix (not saved for the one-source-writing-case) has to do something with it, but I am not fully sure. 

In any case, saving the entire skymodel does not seem to work either, as the to_yaml() makes a call to to_dict() which checks for nan values in the skymodel. For Fermi, there are several spatial parameters (lon_0, lat_0, etc) with nans in the min, max for instance. The error while saving the full model in that case is

```
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

**Expected behavior**
I would expect to get the same plot, and to be able to write the model to disk despite the nan values in some parameters.

**To Reproduce**
```
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

plot_kwargs = {
    "energy_bounds": [1*u.GeV,300*u.GeV], 
    "sed_type": "e2dnde",
    "yunits": u.Unit("erg cm-2 s-1"),
    "xunits": u.GeV,
    "ax": ax,
}

LAT_FullDataset.model['mysource'].spectral_model.plot_error(**plot_kwargs,
                                         label='LAT Full Dataset',
                                         ec='C0',facecolor='white',hatch='+++++++',alpha=0.5,zorder=-10)

myloadedmodel = Models.from_yaml(Models(LAT_FullDataset.model['mysource']).to_yaml())
myloadedmodel['mysource'].spectral_model.plot_error(**plot_kwargs,
                                         label='LAT Full Dataset',
                                         ec='C3',facecolor='white',hatch='\\\\\\\\\\',alpha=0.5,zorder=-10)

```

```
    def _set_norm_parameter(self, norm=None, scaled_parameter=None):
        """Define properties of the norm spectral parameter."""
        if norm is None:
            norm = Parameter("norm", 1, unit="", interp="log")

        norm.value = 1.0
        norm.frozen = False

        norm.min = scaled_parameter.min / scaled_parameter.value
        norm.max = scaled_parameter.max / scaled_parameter.value
        norm.interp = scaled_parameter.interp
        norm.scan_values = self.norm_values
        norm.scan_min = self.norm_min
        norm.scan_max = self.norm_max
        norm.scan_n_values = self.norm_n_values
        return norm
```

I believe some if is missing there, to catch the case where norm is not provided (None value), in which case some sort of default attributes for norm are given.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1521523122/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

1.0.1

**Bug description**

In visualization/utils.py I see

```
L90: plot_kwargs.setdefault("edgecolor", props.pop("color"))
```

**Expected behavior**
before popping 'color' we should make sure props include it.

Long run dataset, running https://docs.gammapy.org/1.0.1/user-guide/makers/reflected.html?highlight=reflected%20regions%20background

correctly","user":{"login":"mireianievas","id":2607760,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MDc3NjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2607760?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas","html_url":"https://github.com/mireianievas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":39740769,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzOTc0MDc2OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"fc2929","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-04-27T11:00:26Z","updated_at":"2023-04-27T11:00:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version**
1.0.1

**Bug description**
Looks like the distances are not handled correctly in ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker.

For example,

```
ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker(
                min_distance = 15 * u.deg,
                max_region_number=n_off_regions,
                min_distance_input = 25*u.deg)
```

I would expect this to result in no OFF regions generated, since the distance between them is huge. However, it results in

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2607760/234841982-9e05a222-0d41-4fba-afca-b79875e52729.png)

If I put arcmins there, that ring of background circles should not be continuous since the distance is not that small, and it should be interrupted near the ON region. 

ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker(
                min_distance = 15 * u.arcmin,
                max_region_number=n_off_regions,
                min_distance_input = 25*u.arcmin)

but it results in

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2607760/234842414-3e9d22b7-0e00-425b-b68a-93aad2bb5566.png)

**Expected behavior**
I would expect the first plot (or something alike) when running the code with the distances of the second example. 

**To Reproduce**

Running ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker specifying min_distance and min_distance_input to some value.

1.0.1

**Bug description**
Looks like the distances are not handled correctly in ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker.

For example,

```
ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker(
                min_distance = 15 * u.deg,
                max_region_number=n_off_regions,
                min_distance_input = 25*u.deg)

If I put arcmins there, that ring of background circles should not be continuous since the distance is not that small, and it should be interrupted near the ON region. 

```
ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker(
                min_distance = 15 * u.arcmin,
                max_region_number=n_off_regions,
                min_distance_input = 25*u.arcmin)
```

but it results in

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2607760/234842414-3e9d22b7-0e00-425b-b68a-93aad2bb5566.png)

**Expected behavior**
I would expect the first plot (or something alike) when running the code with the distances of the second example. 

**To Reproduce**

Running ReflectedRegionsBackgroundMaker specifying min_distance and min_distance_input to some value.

{"action":"opened","number":178,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/enrico/pulls/178","id":1286137982,"node_id":"PR_kwDOACyaMc5MqOh-","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/enrico/pull/178","diff_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/enrico/pull/178.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/enrico/pull/178.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/enrico/issues/178","number":178,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Handle complex multi-component sources in XML model generator","user":{"login":"mireianievas","id":2607760,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MDc3NjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2607760?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas","html_url":"https://github.com/mireianievas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Before: 

- Sources that are very close: assume it is the target source and add it at the beginning of the XML with free parameters. 
- Problem: Crab in 4FGL has Crab Nebula sync + Crab Nebula IC + Crab pulsar as 3 sources. Enrico analysis is done in just a single source (Crab 'total').

Now

- If the input srcname is in the 4FGL, then that is the target source (e.g. J0534.5+2201i --> Crab Nebula IC). Other co-spatial sources (e.g. J0534.5+2201s or J0534.5+2200 --> Crab Nebula Sync and Crab Pulsar) are added too, but with FIXED parameters.
- If the input srcname is not in the 4FGL, do the 'total' analysis with a single source as before. # Default config and validation file for Enrico.

	diffuse_iso_dir = ""
	diffuse_gal = ""

	#Freeze the spectral index of the source. Has no implication if 0 (Left free to vary)

	WriteCovMatrix = yes
	#Write residuals to csv file

	#Assumed Spectral index

	TSlimit = 25.0

	DiagnosticPlots = yes
	# Plot (update) the parent config related plots during time bin production
	DiagnosticParentPlots = no

	#Number of bins for the orbitally folded LC
	NLCbin = 10
	#Epoch of phase=0 in MJD, equal to tmin is 0

[AppLC]
	#Generates fits files or not?

	#Spectral index for the exposure calculation

	#Apperture radius
	rad = 1.0
	#Number of bins
	NLCbin = 10
	#bin form data or frozen bin size
	binsFromData = no
	#minimum energy for the apperture lightcurve, if -1 use the one define in energy section
	emin = -1.0
	emax = -1.0

[Ebin]
	#Generates fits files or not?
	FitsGeneration = yes
	NumEnergyBins = 15
	#Compute an UL if the TS of the sources is <TSEnergyBins
	TSEnergyBins = 9.0
	# How to generate the Ebins
	# - logE: log scaled bins

	# - mix: half-way between log and TS.
	# - [ E1, E2, E3, E4, ..., EN ]: custom binning. 
	# - default is logE
	DistEbins = logE

[TSMap]
	#Re-fit before computing the TS map
	Re-Fit = no
	#Numbers of pixel in x and y
	npix = 10
	#Remove or not the target from the model
	RemoveTarget = yes
	#Generate the TS map pixel by pixel or by grouping the pixels by row.

	method = row

[findsrc]
	#Generates fits files or not?
	FitsGeneration = yes
	#Reoptimize before
	Refit = yes

[srcprob]
	#Generates fits files or not?
	FitsGeneration = yes
	#radius for the computation
	rad = 1.0
	# list of sources
	srclist = ""
	# number of photons to print
	numberPhoton = 10

[Contours]
	parname1 = Prefactor
	parname2 = Index

[ComponentAnalysis]
	# EUnBinned to the energy (MeV) where

	EUnBinned = -1.0
	# Divide the analysis in FrontBack classes
	FrontBack = no
	# Divide the analysis in PSF classes
	PSF = no
	# Divide the analysis in EDISP classes
	EDISP = no
	# Divide the analysis in EDISP classes
	FGL4 = no

[ObservationSimulation]
	infile = Simmodel.xml

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The current stable branch of gammapy supports CircleSkyRegion 1D + 3D datasets. I have tested only the 1D but plan to do the same for 3D. However, the 1D analysis only supports CircleSkyRegion styled datasets (the ones with fixed rad_max). The PointSkyRegion styled datasets (energy-dependent rad_max) does not seem to be supported. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Allow for PointSkyRegion styled datasets (energy-dependent rad_max) to be analyzed as well. 

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
I am not fully sure why we need PointSkyRegions in the first place. Wouldn't an unified SkyRegion type be enough for this (maybe just using a very small radius when needed?).

","user":{"login":"mireianievas","id":2607760,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2MDc3NjA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2607760?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas","html_url":"https://github.com/mireianievas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mireianievas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":39740769,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzOTc0MDc2OQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"fc2929","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/milestones/29","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/milestone/29","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/milestones/29/labels","id":7828787,"node_id":"MI_kwDOAJm2KM4Ad3Uz","number":29,"title":"1.0","description":"","creator":{"login":"registerrier","id":5047091,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwNDcwOTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5047091?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier","html_url":"https://github.com/registerrier","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":31,"closed_issues":130,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-04-01T13:51:47Z","updated_at":"2022-10-07T16:07:07Z","due_on":"2022-10-07T07:00:00Z","closed_at":null},"comments":20,"created_at":"2022-09-29T10:48:49Z","updated_at":"2022-10-11T10:02:31Z","closed_at":"2022-10-04T00:33:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. {/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/registerrier/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":30,"closed_issues":130,"state":"open","created_at":"2022-04-01T13:51:47Z","updated_at":"2022-10-11T15:00:04Z","due_on":"2022-10-07T07:00:00Z","closed_at":null},"comments":24,"created_at":"2022-09-29T10:48:49Z","updated_at":"2022-10-11T16:25:40Z","closed_at":"2022-10-04T00:33:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Gammapy version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. Right now it is completely silent I think, it is not straightforward to check if the data is being used at all. But I personally think this is not how it should be. In a Point-like / 1D analysis, at least at this late stage of the analysis, position accuracy is not that relevant. One knows which source is being observed and in this particular case, even if LST is managing internally a source region a few arcsecs off from that of LAT, that should make no difference and the summed likelihood should be allowed. version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. Points do not include anything. return in_reg else: return np.logical_not(in_reg) ``` Knowing it is a bit of workaround, perhaps I could 'trick' that call setting that meta = {'include' = False} ? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. """ node_type = "label" def __init__(self, labels, name=""): unique_labels = set(labels) if not len(unique_labels) == len(labels): raise ValueError("Node labels must be unique") self._labels = np.array(labels) self._name = name ``` why do we convert from a list of str into a set? just to get the unique ones? why not doing a np.unique(labels) and then we do not need to do the conversion into np.array() ? **Description**
<!-- What is the motivation for this pull request? -->
<!-- Briefly: what changes are done here? -->
<!-- If this is to address a Github issue, mention its number to create a link -->
<!-- Example: This PR is to fix issue #42 -->

This pull request addresses the issues raised in #4117. It includes the following checks:
- Check that the dataset name is a `str`
- Modify the behavior for `RegionGeom.contains` and `PointSkyRegion`. The implementation in `regions`, does not include the coordinate even if it is exactly at the center of the region. The point sky region here is approximated by a rectangular region of the size of 1x1 pixel.

version** 0.20.1 Are you using the latest stable version of Gammapy? (see https://gammapy.org/news.html) yes **Bug description** Issue discussed already in slack (and I should probably have opened this ticket back then, my bad). A 3D (e.g. Fermi) and a 1D ON/OFF datasets (PointSkyRegion type, e.g. LST or MAGIC) are analyzed together with N skymodels for the 3D (with spatial and spectral components, including those for the source of interest) and 1 skymodel (the same model as in the 3D for ONLY the source of interest, including spectral and spatial components). The result, after the likelihood fit, is that one of the datasets is completely ignored in the fit. However, no error or warning is raised, so I have no idea how to debug it. Partial workaround: 1) define a skymodel for the 1D dataset consisting only on a spectral component, no spatial ones. 2) The parameters of the spectral components are linked/fixed to the corresponding spectral component of the source model in the 3D dataset (lets call the source name in the 3D dataset XXX). 3) The source name of the skymodel then needs to be different, e.g. renamed to XXX-2, otherwise gammapy returns an error saying that the names should be unique. 4) perform the fit, and then voila both datasets contribute to the result, the confidence band is much narrower. However, this is not a perfect solution, because the FluxPointEstimator needs to be called for each dataset with their unique source names (even when it is the same source and both datasets should contribute to the energy ranges where both instruments are sensitive). So you have Fermi points on one hand and IACT points on the other. At the overlapping energy range (~20-300 GeV) you may have 'duplicated' points with larger error bars (because only one dataset contributes at a time). **Expected behavior** The first option, i.e. defining 'datasets' to include both the 3D and the 1D (PointSkyRegion) and then making the fit should work fine, with the only difference with respect to a 3D that for the 1D model the spatial component should not contribute to the likelihood. Or if there is any reason why it shouldn't, it should at least raise an error instead of silently ignoring one of the datasets. Another possible solution would be to try to get the FluxPointEstimation to also use the two datasets to compute each flux point, but that would involve dealing with two datasets with a source with different names in each. If the global fit works this way, no reason why the flux point estimation proceedure, which basically re-optimizes the 'norm' in each bin, shouldn't. But it sounds more 'hacky' to me than just fixing the first option and allowing the minimizer to work with this 1D+3D datasets **To Reproduce** 1) Build a 3D dataset (suggested from a Fermi analysis done with the Fermitools). 2) Build a 1D dataset (energy-dependent / PointSkyRegion style, I ignore if the standard CircleSkyRegion analysis reproduces the issue) 3) Assign all the spatial+spectral skymodels for each source in the field of the 3D dataset. 4) Assign the spatial+spectral for the source of interest to match the one in the 3D dataset. 5) Build the datasets([1D,3D]) object. 6) Make the likelihood fit with the models **Other information** Any other information you think will be useful for us to fix the issue can go here. - Adapt to the newest 4FGL-DR3. A couple of variables changed names (added trailing S, I'm unsure if it was intentional or a typo, Jean Ballet has been contacted).
